narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as a bit too rocky—landing at a 51% chance of not passing. Picture it like a story of your journey so far: your Year 3 grades placed you in the 2nd decile at school, which stood out as the biggest signal of concern, suggesting the rigors of advanced coursework have been a real challenge. That low LSAT score of 30 echoed that struggle, painting a picture of hurdles in the kind of high-stakes testing the bar demands. Even your Year 1 decile of 2 reinforced this early pattern, like the opening chapters setting a tough tone. Coming from a middle-income family added another layer, as we've seen it correlate with extra pressures that can tip the scales toward difficulty. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.4 fit right into this narrative, showing consistent academic climbs that haven't quite reached the heights needed for bar success. As a white female studying part-time, these details together nudged the story further toward potential stumbles, based on patterns from thousands of similar paths. It's not that any one thing doomed you—it's this combination that made the model pause, whispering that without some big shifts, the bar might slip away. But here's the empowering part of your story: you're the author, and with targeted boosts like LSAT prep or stronger study strategies, you could rewrite the ending. Many in your shoes have turned it around—what's one step you'll take next? We're rooting for your comeback.
